name: The stilling of the storm
notes: All three Synoptics recount Jesus crossing the lake with his disciples in a ship, asleep through a great tempest until they wake him crying that they perish; he arises, rebukes the wind and the sea, and there is a great calm, then asks after their fear and faith while they marvel, 'What manner of man is this, that even the winds and the sea obey him.' The shared core is firm and is linked pair-by-pair. Each account keeps its own vantage: Mark alone notes the even, the other little ships, the pillow in the hinder part, and the command 'Peace, be still'; Luke alone names the lake, the launching forth, and that they were in jeopardy; Matthew alone calls it a great tempest and frames the cry as 'Lord, save us.' These complementary vantages enrich one another and are left in place where they are not shared.
